{{COURSE_CODE}} / {{COURSE_NAME}}
Materials Student guide About TDAA
For instructors · One-time setup

Set up the harness.

A step-by-step recipe for bringing up the TDAA-Go harness on a new machine and producing your first week of materials. Roughly one hour end-to-end. Assumes a textbook PDF, a GitHub account, and a terminal.

Looking for student-facing study guidance?

This page documents the instructor side: forking the template, bootstrapping a course, and producing weekly materials. For the student-facing weekly workflow — read, prompt an AI, validate, test — see the student guide.

In this guide
  1. 01 Install the prerequisites
  2. 02 Fork the TDAA-Go repository
  3. 03 Bootstrap the course metadata
  4. 04 Generate the first week
  5. 05 Build and preview locally
  6. 06 Publish to a student-facing website
  7. 07 Iterate weekly
  8. 08 Grade submitted work
§ 01

Install the prerequisites

The harness needs four things installed once per machine: Typst to compile the course documents, uv for the bootstrap scripts, the GitHub CLI for creating and pushing repositories, and an AI coding assistant such as Claude Code.

On macOS with Homebrew:

brew install typst gh uv               # core tools
brew install --cask claude-code        # AI assistant

On other platforms, install Typst from typst.app, uv from docs.astral.sh/uv, the GitHub CLI from github.com/cli/cli, and Claude Code (or Codex, Cursor) from the respective vendor pages.

After installing the GitHub CLI, sign in once:

gh auth login  # sign in to GitHub
§ 02

Fork the repository

The reusable harness lives in GiggleLiu/TDAA-Go. Create a fresh private copy under your own GitHub account. Replace my-course with a short folder name such as automata-2026: 

gh repo create my-course --private --template GiggleLiu/TDAA-Go --clone
cd my-course

This produces a clean folder with document templates, website templates, the GitHub Pages deployment workflow, build commands, AI-assisted skills, and example configuration files — but none of the weekly content from any reference course.

§ 03

Bootstrap the course metadata

Open the fresh repository in the AI coding assistant:

claude  # start assistant

Then run the bootstrap skill:

> /bootstrap

The skill first asks whether you have existing course materials. If so, drop syllabi, slides, exams, or notes into the reference/ folder. /bootstrap runs uv sync, converts supported PDF / PPTX / DOCX / Markdown / text files into reference/*.md, and extracts course metadata from them with source and confidence notes. If you have no such materials, it asks directly for course code, name, textbook author/title/edition, week count, instructor name, and institution.

From these inputs /bootstrap produces:

  • config.toml — editable course metadata (the config.typ shim only loads and re-exports it)
  • Substituted course-level placeholders in .github/templates/*.html
  • textbook/*.md — extracted definitions, theorems, proofs, and examples from the textbook PDF
  • coursedesign/schedule.typ — a draft week-by-week section assignment
Read before continuing

The textbook extraction and the proposed schedule should be read and corrected by hand. AI extraction is good enough to start from, but not good enough to ship.

§ 04

Generate the first week

With the schedule in place, generate week 1:

> /generate-week 1

The skill runs the writer-reviewer debate for the learning sheet, then a similar pass for the test files. The main output is a weekly bundle under week1/: 1.learning-sheet.typ, 1.test.typ, 1.test.B.typ, and 1.validation.typ.

If you want finer control, the smaller skills can be used individually: /write-learning-sheet, /review-learning-sheet, /revise, /write-tests, and /review-tests.

§ 05

Build and preview locally

Compile learning sheets into the course website, or open the local preview. Use make build for a quick check; make serve when you want it in a browser: 

make build  # compile learning-sheet PDFs and site
make serve  # preview at http://localhost:8000

This builds _site/ with learning-sheet PDFs, viewer pages, and this setup guide. make serve keeps running while the preview is open; press Ctrl-C to stop. Tests and validation sets are compiled with separate targets: 

make compile-tests   # compile test PDFs
make dump-solutions  # compile test PDFs with solutions visible
Review before release

Common issues to fix at this stage: a test that references a term not defined in the learning sheet; validation and test files whose question count or point distribution do not match; AI prompts that are too narrow (deeper only, never broader).

§ 06

Publish to a student-facing site

Ask the assistant to save the reviewed materials and push them to GitHub. TDAA-Go already includes the website templates and Pages workflow, and /bootstrap attempts to enable Pages automatically when gh is authenticated and you have repository permission: 

> Commit and push the week 1 materials.

The assistant should show the files it is about to include, create a commit, and push to GitHub. After the push, the GitHub Pages workflow publishes the student-facing website.

If Pages was not enabled automatically: in the repo, open Settings → Pages and set Source to GitHub Actions. The included deploy-pages.yml workflow rebuilds and republishes on every push to main that touches .typ, .html, config.toml, the Makefile, or the workflow itself.

§ 07

Iterate weekly

For each subsequent week the cycle is the same generation-review-build sequence followed by a push. For week 2: 

> /generate-week 2
> /revise 2
make build  # rebuild site

Then ask the assistant:

> Commit and push week 2.

For week 3, change 2 to 3, and so on. Run make serve first if you want a local preview before pushing.

Optional · Zulip drip release

If you set zulip-stream in config.toml and add the ZULIP_BOT_EMAIL, ZULIP_BOT_API_KEY, and ZULIP_SITE repo secrets, the hourly release-materials.yml workflow drip-releases learning sheets, validation answers, and test answers per a schedule in coursedesign/release-schedule.json. Leave zulip-stream empty to disable the workflow entirely.

§ 08

Grade submitted work

When students submit written work as a mixed folder of PDF, JPG, or DOCX files, the harness provides two grading skills. /grade-homework discovers submissions, compares them to a teacher-provided solution document with point allocations, confirms the rubric before grading, and produces grades/grades.csv plus per-student feedback with explicit flags on items that need teacher review. /homework-report then builds a teacher-facing PDF report that shows each student's submission images beside the per-question marks and feedback: 

> /grade-homework
> /homework-report
{{COURSE_CODE}} · {{INSTITUTION}} Built with Typst, scaffolded by TDAA-Go.
